notes.anchor: PLANCVS IMR COS. DESIG. S. D. COS. PR. TR. PL. SENATVI POPVLO PLEBIQVE ROMANAE
notes.note: The formal salutation of an official senatorial dispatch: Plancus writes as imperator (battlefield commander's acclamation, abbrev. IMR) and consul-designate (COS. DESIG.) to the consuls (COS.), praetor (PR.), tribunes of the plebs (TR. PL.), Senate, and Roman people --- the full constitutional address for a public letter read aloud in the Senate house.

notes.anchor: the easiest road of the common safety rather than that of my own renown
notes.note: Plancus presents strategic delay as a selfless choice: by waiting to gather strength before committing openly, he pursued the public good (communis salus) at the cost of personal gloria, a pointed reversal of the usual Roman political calculus.

notes.anchor: several communities, which the year before had been bound by largesses and grants of rewards
notes.note: Antony had in 44 BC distributed land grants, cash, and privileges to communities in Gaul to secure their loyalty; Plancus describes his diplomatic work to detach them from those obligations.

notes.anchor: from my colleague's case, how dangerous to a good citizen unprepared is the premature unveiling of his hand
notes.note: Plancus's consul-designate colleague was Lepidus, whose peace-advocacy letters to the Senate had already damaged his standing; Plancus implies he learned from Lepidus's exposed and ambiguous position that premature declaration was politically fatal.

notes.anchor: C. Furnius, my legate --- a man of courage and energy
notes.note: Gaius Furnius served as Plancus's senior legate (legatus) in Transalpine Gaul and was repeatedly dispatched to Rome as a personal emissary; he is also the recipient of favourable mention by Cicero throughout the Fam. 10 correspondence.

notes.anchor: I have five legions under the standards
notes.note: Transalpine Gaul's garrison of five legions made Plancus one of the most powerful military actors outside Italy in early 43; this force was what Cicero and the Senate were urgently trying to commit to the anti-Antony cause.
